1: Nebuchadnezzar to all peoples, nations, and languages that dwell in all the earth: Peace be multiplied to you. The king's letter-decree begins with a universal address — the same scope as the gospel proclamation (Mark 16:15: go into all the world and proclaim the gospel to the whole creation). Nebuchadnezzar's letter narrates his conversion; the proclamation of Christ goes further — it transforms nations rather than merely reporting a king's experience.
2: It has seemed good to me to show the signs and wonders that the Most High God has done for me. The personal testimony as a form of witness — the king narrating what God has done for him. This is the testimony structure of the early church: Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:22-36), Paul before Agrippa (Acts 26:2-23), the Samaritan woman (John 4:28-29). The transformed life becomes evidence of divine power.
3: How great are his signs, how mighty his wonders! His kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and his dominion endures from generation to generation. The doxology of a converted pagan king — echoing the language of the Psalms (Psalms 145:13 — your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom). This verse's confession is exactly what the nations will declare when every knee bows at the name of Jesus (Philippians 2:10-11; Revelation 11:15).
4: I, Nebuchadnezzar, was at ease in my house and prospering in my palace. The posture of complacency — security and prosperity as the platform from which pride grows. The parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21: the ground of a rich man produced plentifully, and he said to himself, Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years) describes the same spiritual danger. Nebuchadnezzar's story is the parable of the rich fool played out in history with a surprise ending.
5: I saw a dream that made me afraid. The dream that disturbs the prosperous king is divine interruption — heaven breaking into the comfortable certainty of earthly power. Christ's words are similarly disruptive: Do not lay up treasures on earth (Matthew 6:19); Sell your possessions and give to the poor (Luke 12:33). The comfortable are disturbed by the word of God so that they may find true security.
6: I made a decree to bring in all the wise men of Babylon that they might make known to me the interpretation. The failure of Babylon's wisdom system — it cannot interpret what God reveals. This is the epistemological point Paul makes in 1 Corinthians 1:20-21: where is the wise man? God made foolish the wisdom of the world. True wisdom requires revelation, not mere learning.
7: The magicians, enchanters, Chaldeans, and astrologers came in, and I told them the dream, but they could not make known to me its interpretation. The complete failure of human wisdom before divine mystery. Paul: the natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are folly to him, and he is not able to understand them because they are spiritually discerned (1 Corinthians 2:14). Only those given the Spirit of God can interpret the dreams of God.
8: At last Daniel came in before me — he in whom is the spirit of the holy gods. The pagans' description of Daniel (spirit of the holy gods) captures something real in pagan language: Daniel is Spirit-endowed in a way that transcends natural human capacity. Paul's description of the Spirit-filled believer resonates: the Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God (1 Corinthians 2:10). Christ promises the Spirit of truth who guides into all truth (John 16:13).
9: O Belteshazzar, chief of the magicians, because I know that the spirit of the holy gods is in you, tell me the visions of my dream. The king approaches Daniel for interpretation — recognition that human wisdom cannot decode divine messages. Christ is the one who interprets the Father to humanity: No one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him (Matthew 11:27). He is not merely an interpreter but the Word himself.
10: The visions of my head as I lay in bed: I saw a tree in the midst of the earth, and its height was great. The great tree visible throughout the whole earth is an image of universal dominion. In the OT, the tree as kingdom-metaphor appears in Ezekiel 17:22-24 (the cedar of Lebanon as the Davidic kingdom) and Ezek 31 (Assyria as a great cedar). Jesus uses a similar image: the mustard seed that becomes a tree in which the birds make nests (Matthew 13:32) — a smaller, hidden-kingdom reversal of Nebuchadnezzar's imperial tree.
11: The tree grew and became strong, and its top reached to heaven, and it was visible to the end of the whole earth. The tree's height — reaching heaven — is the image of Babel (Genesis 11:4: let us build a city whose tower reaches to the heavens). The hubris of empire always reaches toward the divine — attempting to secure what only God can give. Christ, by contrast, descended from heaven (John 6:38) rather than building toward it, reversing the Babel-pride pattern.
12: Its leaves were beautiful and its fruit abundant, and in it was food for all. The beasts found shade under it, and the birds of the air lived in its branches, and all flesh was fed from it. The beneficent empire sheltering and feeding all nations — the tree as the symbol of Pax Babylonica. Jesus uses the same image for the kingdom of heaven (Matthew 13:32: the birds of the air come and make nests in its branches) — but the mustard-seed kingdom grows from the bottom up, not imposed from the top down.
13: A watcher, a holy one, came down from heaven. The heavenly watchers (irin, Aramaic) are angelic beings who monitor earthly affairs and execute divine decrees. Their existence explains the cosmic dimension of political history: behind every human empire there is a heavenly reckoning. Christ himself is the ultimate watcher who descends from heaven — not to observe but to save (John 3:17 — God did not send the Son to condemn the world but that the world might be saved through him).
14: Cut down the tree and lop off its branches. The divine decree of humiliation for the proud tree. Christ's parables include the cutting down of the fruitless fig tree (Luke 13:7-9: cut it down; why should it use up the ground?) and the unfruitful vine branch (John 15:2 — he removes every branch that does not bear fruit). The divine gardener prunes and cuts; Nebuchadnezzar's cutting is the paradigmatic pruning of human pride.
15: Let his portion be with the beasts in the grass of the earth. Let his mind be changed from a man's, and let a beast's mind be given to him. The king reduced to the level of animal — the image that humans are made in (Genesis 1:26-27) exchanged for the image of the beast. Paul describes the same exchange in the fallen human condition: they exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images resembling mortal man and birds and animals (Romans 1:23). Nebuchadnezzar's madness is the externalized form of the internal exchange that idolatry makes in every human heart.
16: Let seven periods of time pass over him. The seven periods (times/seasons) of humiliation — a complete cycle of divine discipline. Christ endured three days in the tomb — the concentrated kairos of divine judgment bearing. Nebuchadnezzar's seven seasons of humiliation are the extended version of what Christ compressed into three days: God's just response to human pride, resolved by divine grace rather than earned recovery.
17: The Most High rules the kingdom of men and gives it to whom he will and sets over it the lowliest of men. The decree of the watchers summarizes the chapter's theology: divine sovereignty over political history. Luke 1:52 — he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate — is the Magnificat's application of the same principle. The kingdom comes to the lowly; Nebuchadnezzar was brought low to receive it.
18: This dream I saw; now you, O Belteshazzar, tell me the interpretation. Daniel is the one in whom the spirit of the holy gods dwells. The appeal to Daniel's Spirit-endowment for interpretation points to Christ as the Spirit-anointed interpreter of divine mysteries (Isaiah 11:2 — the Spirit of wisdom and understanding shall rest upon him). All true prophetic interpretation derives from the Spirit whom Christ sends (John 16:13-14).
19: Daniel was dismayed for a while, and his thoughts alarmed him. The faithful counselor who receives difficult news for a powerful person: the message is true but will cost the king who hears it. Christ wept over Jerusalem before pronouncing its judgment (Luke 19:41-44) — grief and love preceding truth-telling. The prophet who brings hard truth to power does so with sorrow, not satisfaction.
20: The tree you saw, which grew and became strong — it is you, O king. The identification of the tree with the person is the interpretive reveal: the empire and its king are one, and the judgment of the tree falls on the man. Nathan's parable to David (2 Samuel 12:7: you are the man) uses the same dramatic revelation: the story is about you. Christ's parables similarly confront the hearer with their own situation — the fig tree, the vineyard tenants, the prodigal son's elder brother.
21: Its top reached to heaven and its fruit was for all, under which beasts found shade and in whose branches birds of the air lived. The image of benevolent imperial power — feeding and sheltering all — is not false, only insufficient. Earthly power can provide genuine goods (Romans 13:4: the governing authority is God's servant for your good) while still being subject to divine correction when pride replaces service.
22: It is you, O king, who have grown and become strong. Your greatness has grown and reaches to heaven, and your dominion to the ends of the earth. The acknowledgment of genuine greatness before the announcement of judgment. God does not minimize what humans have achieved; he shows where achievement tips into self-worship. The Sermon on the Mount builds on the genuine goods of the OT law before deepening them — Jesus does not deny righteousness but exceeds it (Matthew 5:17-20).
23: A watcher, a holy one, came down from heaven, saying: cut down the tree and destroy it, but leave the stump and its roots in the ground. The preservation of the stump is the key — the cut-down empire is not annihilated but pruned to a root that can be restored. The Isaianic stump of Jesse (Isaiah 11:1 — a shoot from the stump of Jesse) is also a stump-and-root image: the dynasty cut down but the root remaining for the new shoot. Both Nebuchadnezzar and Israel can be restored from their stumps.
24: This is the interpretation, O king: it is a decree of the Most High which has come upon my lord the king. Daniel does not soften the judgment: the decree has come. The finality of divine speech — when God has decreed, it is determined. Yet the finality that falls on Nebuchadnezzar is not without hope (the stump remains). The cross is the decree of the Most High that fell on Christ — final, determined, yet the stump of resurrection remains.
25: You shall be driven from among men, and your dwelling shall be with the beasts of the field. You shall eat grass like an ox, and be wet with the dew of heaven, and seven periods of time shall pass over you, till you know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men and gives it to whom he will. The goal of the humiliation is knowledge — the same goal that the entire exile was designed to achieve for Israel: know that I am the Lord (Ezekiel 36:23-38). Christ's cross achieves the ultimate knowledge: this is how God loves the world (John 3:16); this is the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24).
26: And as it was commanded to leave the stump and roots of the tree, your kingdom shall be confirmed for you from the time that you know that Heaven rules. The condition of restoration is knowing. True knowledge — the experiential recognition of divine sovereignty — restores what pride had forfeited. Christ is the way and the truth and the life (John 14:6): knowing him is not merely intellectual but the transformative knowing that restores the image of God in humanity.
27: Therefore, O king, let my counsel be acceptable to you: break off your sins by practicing righteousness, and your iniquities by showing mercy to the oppressed. The ethical call before the judgment falls — repentance is still possible. This is the pattern of all prophetic preaching including Christ's: Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand (Matthew 4:17). Grace offers the path of repentance before the decree is executed.
28: All this came upon King Nebuchadnezzar. The delay between the warning and the judgment (twelve months, Daniel 4:29) is the grace period — YHWH is slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love (Psalms 103:8). The patience of God waiting for repentance (2 Peter 3:9 — not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance) is demonstrated here: twelve months between warning and judgment, time for Nebuchadnezzar to change.
29: At the end of twelve months he was walking on the roof of the royal palace of Babylon. The moment of the fall: the king at the peak of his empire, surveying his domain, pride filling the twelve-month grace period unrepented. Compare Uzziah who was strong but proud, entering the temple to burn incense — the moment of transgression at the height of power (2 Chronicles 26:16-21).
30: Is not this great Babylon, which I have built by my mighty power as a royal residence and for the glory of my majesty? The words of supreme pride — the first-person construction (I have built, my power, my majesty) identifying the king with the city he rules. Christ never speaks this way: my teaching is not mine but his who sent me (John 7:16); the Father who dwells in me does his works (John 14:10). The one truly great does not claim greatness for himself.
31: While the words were still in the king's mouth, there fell a voice from heaven. The immediate divine response — the words still forming on the lips when the decree falls. The instantaneous judgment on presumptuous pride is the theme of Ananias and Sapphira (Acts 5:1-11) — the lie about the Spirit answered immediately by death. What was spoken against God is answered by what God speaks.
32: The kingdom has departed from you, and you shall be driven from among men, and your dwelling shall be with beasts. The decree executed exactly as announced. Christ's judgment is similarly exact: the cup I drink you will drink (Mark 10:39); the measure you use will be measured to you (Matthew 7:2). Divine justice corresponds precisely to human action.
33: Immediately the word was fulfilled against Nebuchadnezzar. He was driven from among men and ate grass like an ox, and his body was wet with the dew of heaven till his hair grew as long as eagles' feathers, and his nails were like birds' claws. The physical transformation — the human reduced to animal — is the externalization of what pride does internally: the image of God exchanged for the image of the beast. The resurrection reversal is the opposite: the human restored to divine image through the one who is the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15).
34: At the end of the days I, Nebuchadnezzar, lifted my eyes to heaven, and my reason returned to me. The turning point is the lifted eye — the gaze directed from the earthly to the heavenly. I blessed the Most High, and praised and honored him who lives forever. The doxology from the restored king: his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom. The restoration of reason and the restoration of worship are simultaneous — genuine sanity includes the acknowledgment of God. The prodigal son's coming to himself (Luke 15:17) uses the same return-of-reason language.
35: All the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing, and he does according to his will among the host of heaven and among the inhabitants of the earth; and none can stay his hand or say to him, What have you done? The absolute sovereignty confession from a converted pagan king is one of the most expansive statements of divine sovereignty in the OT — surpassing many of the psalms in its comprehensiveness. Paul echoes it in Romans 9:20: who are you, O man, to answer back to God? The cross is the supreme demonstration of this sovereignty: the crucifixion of the Son enacted according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God (Acts 2:23).
36: At the same time my reason returned to me, and for the glory of my kingdom my majesty and splendor returned to me. My counselors and lords sought me out, and I was established in my kingdom and still more greatness was added to me. The restoration exceeds the original: more greatness was added. Job 42:10-12 follows the same pattern — Job's restoration doubled what he had before. Christ's resurrection and exaltation was not a return to pre-incarnate status but an elevation beyond it: exalted at the right hand of the Father with humanity taken into the divine life (Hebrews 2:9 — crowned with glory and honor).
37: Now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and extol and honor the King of heaven, for all his works are right and his ways are just; and those who walk in pride he is able to humble. The final verse's double movement — praise for the King of heaven, warning about pride — is the gospel's own structure: grace for the humble, resistance for the proud (James 4:6; 1 Peter 5:5). The converted king becomes the preacher of the gospel's central paradox: the way up is down; the way to the throne is through the furnace and the field.
